summary: The era of historically low interest rates created data teams that operated as 'performative theater' with unclear mandates and questionable value delivery. Now that money is expensive again, data teams must shift from 'growth at all costs' to proving concrete business impact through the only two levers that matter: making money and reducing costs.
key_points: Low interest rates fueled the biggest asset bubble in history, enabling companies to hire data teams 'just because' without clear mandates
Many data teams were created as performative theater to impress investors rather than deliver business value
The new reality requires data teams to focus on two levers: revenue generation and cost reduction
Teams that cannot demonstrate measurable business impact will be dissolved
Constraints and scarcity sharpen focus in ways that abundance never could
money_quotes: Abundance has a way of distracting people into the hallucination of progress. On the other hand, constraints and scarcity have a way of sharpening the mind.
With direction elusive, often data teams were quite a bit like performative theater, doing experimental 'data stuff' but not contributing to business impact.
If your data team was removed, would the business be negatively impacted? If the answer is no, you need to start looking for a new job since you're probably not the first to ask this question.
In the good old days, data teams had to figure out how to grow at all costs. These data teams need to figure out how to survive at all costs.
key_insight: The cheap money era allowed data teams to exist as performative theater without delivering real value, but rising interest rates are forcing a return to fundamentals where only teams that measurably impact revenue or costs will survive.
